summary: I was born in a small country village in Ashimori, Kirigun, Okayama Prefecture, Japan in 1896. I attended elementary school, junior high, and one year of girls high school there before entering mid-wife school. My early life and education were deeply rooted in Ashimori, where my family lived and worked.

My family practiced Nichiren Buddhism, and my grandfather often told me the life story of Nichiren Shouin, urging me to believe in him. Growing up, Nichiren's teachings were a significant part of my childhood as my grandfather shared them repeatedly. This religious background shaped my early spiritual life before I encountered Christianity.

I was first exposed to Christianity while working at the Prefecture Hospital in Japan, where a Christian head nurse took me to hear a Caucasian missionary speaking fluent Japanese. Although I did not convert before marriage, after coming to America and especially during WWII internment, I began attending Christian services in Japanese. Eventually, I was baptized in camp, encouraged by ministers and my family, and Christianity became a great source of strength for me and my husband.

My husband was baptized in a Catholic Church because he started taking English courses at Gonzaga College in Spokane. This experience influenced his spiritual path and gave him strength throughout his life. Gonzaga played a role in his early American education and religious journey.

While I was attending English classes in the internment camp, Mrs. Iwatsuki from Hood River, Oregon encouraged me to receive baptism. Her words helped me decide to become a Christian, and I was baptized shortly after. Hood River was connected to my spiritual awakening during the difficult camp years.

My family and I were evacuated to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center during WWII. It was there that I began attending Christian services in Japanese and eventually was baptized. Tule Lake was a place of hardship but also spiritual growth for me and my family during the war.

After leaving the internment camp, my husband worked in Spokane where the railroad company’s main office was located. We bought a home there after our son was killed in the war. Spokane became our home and the place where my family tried to rebuild after the war.

My elder daughter was relocated to Washington, D.C. during the war while the rest of us were at Tule Lake and later Spokane. Although I was not with her, I remember that she was part of our family’s dispersal during the difficult war years. Washington, D.C. was a distant but important place for my family.

My son was inducted into the United States Army after attending college for one year. He was sent overseas after only a few weeks of training and was killed in action just four months after induction. His service and sacrifice were a great sorrow for me but also a source of pride.

I was shocked when I heard that Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941. I was watching television and could tell from the excitement that something serious had happened. This event marked the beginning of a difficult time for my family and all Japanese Americans.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: I was also shocked when America dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Although it was a tragic event, I believed Japan had to accept the consequences because they started the war. These bombings, along with the war, led me to find God and become a Christian.

My maiden name was Kawata. I was born into the Kawata family in Ashimori, Japan, and my father was an adopted son-in-law who had come to America when I was nine. The Kawata family background shaped much of my early life and values.

My father came to America and lived in California where he rented a place and farmed with my brother. Many Japanese came to California to work and save money to invest back home. Although my father did not become wealthy, he managed to get by working there.

I arrived in America at Seattle, Washington by the ship Hawaii Maru. Seattle was the port of entry for me when I came to join my husband, who was working in Harrington, Washington. It was the beginning of my life in America.

My husband was working as a railroad foreman in Harrington, Washington when I arrived in America. Harrington was a small town about 250 miles from Seattle where we lived in a company house. It was the place where my American life began and where I adapted to a new culture.

Before the war broke out, we were living in Monroe, Washington, about 40 miles from Seattle. We were thinking of buying a home there for our son, but after he was killed in action, we decided to move to Spokane instead. Monroe was part of our life before the war disrupted everything.

During the Great Depression, my husband's railroad section was divided and his salary was cut in half. Despite the cut, we managed to get by because he still earned more than many others in our small town. It was a difficult time but we were fortunate compared to others.

I came to America aboard the ship Hawaii Maru, which arrived in Seattle. On the ship were other picture brides, some meeting their husbands for the first time. My journey on the Hawaii Maru marked the start of my new life in the United States.
